text:
FormerManchester UnitedbossSir Alex Fergusonwas on the brink of managingTeam GBat theLondon 2012 Olympics.That's according to Lord Sebastian Coe, who was chair of the London Organising Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games at the time.

Speaking to The Sun, Coe said they "got very close" to securing Ferguson for the role after numerous discussions during the 2007/08 season. He also shared his belief that no club would object to their Under-23 players receiving a six-week masterclass from Ferguson.

Coe added that he had approached Bob Charlton about the idea, who promised to get Ferguson to call him. The Scot finally called a few weeks later while Coe was in Cobham Tesco, answering before explaining his proposition. He added: "I did explain what my theory was because Bob hadn't actually told him. He'd just asked him to give me a call."

Coe continued: "So Alex rang and said: 'Oh, I thought you were looking for tickets'. I put him through the idea and he said: 'Well, I don't know.' Then there was a gap and then he went: 'Oh Jesus, I'm already picking the team in my head'".

The two would meet in person at theBBCreview of the year in 2007, with Coe going over to Ferguson and congratulating him on giving Bobby Robson a Lifetime Achievement Award. It was here where Coe was told "yes" by Sir Alex to the exciting opportunity, after much reflection.

He added: "Alex looked at me and said: 'The answer's yes.' I said fine. We never said anymore other than that we'd speak again." In the end, the British Olympic Association decided that at the time England Under-21s boss Stuart Pearce was the right man for the job, with Ferguson carrying on withManchester Uniteduntil he retired from management in 2013.



Lord Coe admitted his regret in failing to secure Ferguson as Olympics boss: "We got pretty close to it. I've always laughed with Alex afterwards. I bump into him fairly regularly. He often wonders whether he missed out on a really good experience," he said.

"The players would have benefited a lot from him. That was one of my disappointments that I never got Alex Ferguson across the line. The BOA decided that Stuart Peace probably had better credentials. That was a slight disappointment."

While Ferguson enjoyed a successful playing career, scoring 171 goals in 317 games for clubs like Queen's Park, Dunfermline and Rangers, he is best known for his legendary spell managingManchester United.At Old Trafford, he secured an incredible 38 trophies - including 13 Premier League titles, five FA Cups and two Champions League titles.

After retiring from playing in 1974, Ferguson began his managerial journey with East Stirlingshire that same year, later spending four years with St Mirren. Following eight years at Aberdeen, he took charge of Scotland at the 1986 World Cup in Mexico after the death of then-manager Jock Stein.

Fergie's stint in international football was brief but memorable, stepping down in June 1986 after Scotland failed to progress from their group with Denmark, West Germany and Uruguay securing just one point from a draw with the latter. The same year, Ferguson joined United, marking the beginning of his legendary 27-year tenure at Old Trafford.

title: Why Alex Ferguson's agreement to manage at Olympic Games fell through | Football | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Aaron Morris
description: Former Manchester United boss Sir Alex Ferguson was close to managing the Team GB men's football team at the London 2012 Olympics, according to Lord Seb Coe.
